channel.title: The Grant Desk - Worldwide, next 90 days
channel.description: Paid open calls, fellowships and residencies in AI, tech, research, and digital and mixed-media arts, sorted into the right pile. Updated as new calls land on the desk. Filtered to: Worldwide, next 90 days.

item.title: Liu Shiming Art Foundation: Artist Grants 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Liu Shiming Art Foundation

Location: Open to artists of any country; application must be in English.

Award: Up to five grants of USD 5,000 each. Disbursed in two payments (USD 3,000 by 15 January 2027 and USD 2,000 on project completion). No application fee.

Duration: Project grant for new work

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

The Liu Shiming Art Foundation's annual Artist Grants (fourth edition) award up to five grants of USD 5,000 each to early-career visual artists to create new work exploring connections between traditional, cultural, or historical influences and contemporary artistic practice. ELIGIBILITY: visual artists in the first decade of their career (two to ten years practicing professionally); applicants must have received a studio-arts degree between 2016 and 2024, or had their debut gallery or juried-exhibition show between 2016 and 2026. Open to artists of any country; the application and all supporting materials must be in English. TIMELINE: applications open 1 June 2026; close 31 August 2026; recipients notified by 2 December 2026. Apply via the Foundation's grants page.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: IIAS: Asia in the World Fellowship Programme 2027-2028
item.description:
Organisation: International Institute for Asian Studies (IIAS), Leiden University

Location: Two phases: a partner institution in Asia, Africa, Latin America, or Europe (Phase 1), then Leiden, Netherlands (Phase 2). Open internationally.

Award: Leiden phase: EUR 1,250 per month stipend plus EUR 1,200 per month housing allowance, plus a travel grant; the Phase 1 stipend at the co-host institution is set by that institution. No application fee.

Duration: 9 months (two phases)

Deadline: 10 Sep 2026

The Asia in the World Fellowship Programme, run by the International Institute for Asian Studies (IIAS) at Leiden University, is a nine-month, two-phase fellowship pairing a residency at a partner institution in Asia, Africa, Latin America, or Europe (Phase 1) with six months in residence at IIAS in Leiden, the Netherlands (Phase 2). The programme supports interdisciplinary, humanities- and social-science-based research engaging Asia and its connections with the wider world. SUPPORT during the Leiden phase: EUR 1,250 per month stipend, EUR 1,200 per month housing allowance, and a travel grant; the Phase 1 stipend is provided by the co-host institution. ELIGIBILITY: scholars and researchers worldwide; open internationally. APPLICATIONS open 1 July 2026; deadline 10 September 2026. Apply via the IIAS fellowships page. NOTE: dates confirmed via secondary sources at time of entry (the funder page was not directly reachable); verify before applying.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Akademie Schloss Solitude: Web Residencies No. 22 - Ignore All Previous Instructions
item.description:
Organisation: Akademie Schloss Solitude

Location: Online (fully remote). Awarded by Akademie Schloss Solitude, Stuttgart, Germany.

Award: EUR 1,200 grant for a four-week online residency, plus a microsite to develop and publish the project, and support from a web/UX designer and a programmer in a supportive capacity.

Duration: Four-week online residency.

Deadline: 07 Aug 2026

Web Residencies No. 22, 'Ignore All Previous Instructions,' is curated by !Mediengruppe Bitnik as part of Akademie Schloss Solitude's Web Residencies program, which since 2016 has supported young talents from the international digital scene as well as artists of all disciplines engaging with web-based practices. For the tenth anniversary of the program, this edition invites artists to create works that challenge extractive digital platforms and AI systems, exploring how to interrupt dominant technological logics, from ad-tech surveillance to prompt injection vulnerabilities, and imagine alternative structures and communication methods. The open call seeks proposals that hijack, glitch, and disrupt rather than comply with existing systems, drawing on strategies like Situationist detournement to map routes through and around the grids we are trapped within. Four to six proposals will be selected. BENEFITS: a four-week online residency; a grant of EUR 1,200; a web space (microsite) on which the project can be developed and published online; support from a web/UX designer and a programmer (in a supportive capacity only, not to build an entire website); and participation in individual and group online events with the curators and Solitude fellows. Offline or hybrid formats (a reading session, panel, exhibition, or curated program) may follow. ELIGIBILITY: artists of all disciplines may apply, with no age limit; submission is free of charge. The residency takes place exclusively online, and selected fellows agree to be available online during the four weeks. TIMELINE: the open call runs from July 7 to August 7, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Mountainfilm: Commitment Grants & Emerging Filmmaker Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: Mountainfilm

Location: Application online; Telluride, Colorado, United States (fellowship trip during the festival). Commitment Grants are open to filmmakers from any country; the Emerging Filmmaker Fellowship requires current U.S. residency.

Award: Over USD 90,000 in total funding. Individual Commitment Grants range from USD 3,000 to 6,000. The Emerging Filmmaker Fellowship adds a yearlong mentorship, a cash award, and a funded trip to Telluride over Memorial Day weekend (travel, lodging, and a festival pass).

Duration: Commitment Grants support projects in post-production; the Emerging Filmmaker Fellowship is a yearlong mentorship.

Deadline: 16 Jul 2026

Mountainfilm's Commitment Grants support documentary filmmakers with projects in post-production, with over USD 90,000 in funding this cycle for films that celebrate adventure, activism, social justice, culture, the environment, and indomitable spirit. Mountainfilm actively encourages diversity behind the camera, and a portion of funding is set aside for two focus areas: projects featuring an LGBTQ+ protagonist or a story about overcoming personal challenges, societal stigmas, and/or community obstacles with a meaningful connection to the outdoors or nature-based pursuits; and projects exploring immigration or migration, or created by immigrant or first-generation immigrant filmmakers. AWARDS: individual Commitment Grants range from USD 3,000 to 6,000. EMERGING FILMMAKER FELLOWSHIP: one early-career filmmaker is selected from the Commitment Grant applicant pool for a yearlong mentorship that includes edit and story consultation, networking opportunities, a cash award, and a funded trip to Telluride during the festival over Memorial Day weekend (travel, lodging, and a Mountainfilm pass, applicable to the year following the award). ELIGIBILITY: Commitment Grants are open to applicants from any country; the Emerging Filmmaker Fellowship accepts applicants from any country but requires current U.S. residency. Post-production projects only, completed, released, or festival-screened projects are not eligible, and impact campaigns are not funded. Prior Commitment Grant recipients should not re-apply for the same project; previously declined projects may re-apply only with substantial changes. REQUIREMENTS: a video sample or trailer of the current project no longer than 10 minutes (password-protected Vimeo links preferred); the screener link must stay active and cannot be changed after submission. The Emerging Filmmaker application additionally requires a director bio, headshot, and cover letter. Recipients are expected to provide progress updates 2-3 times a year, include Mountainfilm recognition in credits, and discuss premiere consideration with the programming team. TIMELINE: applications are open July 1 to July 16, 2026 (closing 11:59 PM MDT), with notifications in late August or early September. Questions: entries@mountainfilm.org.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Ecoslay Mag: Open Call for Contributions, Issue 02 "The Urban Issue"
item.description:
Organisation: Ecoslay Mag

Location: Remote / international. Two online workshops with the editorial team; contributions delivered remotely.

Award: Financial compensation for selected contributions (amount unspecified). Editorial support, proofreading, and graphic design provided. No application fee.

Duration: Contributions developed approx. 1 September 2026 to 10 January 2027, with two online workshops (first around 15 September 2026). Final contribution deadline approx. 10 January 2027.

Deadline: 13 Jul 2026

Ecoslay Mag invites contributions for the second print issue of Ecoslay, "The Urban Issue," exploring the city as a space where intersectional ecological futures are negotiated, contested, resisted, and reinvented. In the Ecoslay way, pop culture and its influence are analysed and hijacked to communicate socio-ecological issues and ignite systemic change: what happens when climate politics meets internet culture, urban ecology meets reality TV, and sustainability discourse collides with camp, irony, humour, and memes. FORMATS (including but not limited to): essays, interviews, science communication, poems, visual art, documentation of (art)works, and fiction. TOPICS: urban climate communication through humour, memes, and pop culture; greenwashing, eco-branding, and sustainable aesthetics in urban contexts; urban farming, more-than-human life, housing crises, displacement and green gentrification, class and the city, maintenance and care work in relation to urbanism, urban environmental justice, and community organizing, and how these relate to pop culture; and reflections on what Ecoslay means in an age of overlapping crises. COMMITMENT: selected participants deliver one contribution to Issue 02 and attend two online workshops with the editorial team and fellow contributors for communal exchange and feedback on proposals and works-in-progress. WHAT'S OFFERED: financial compensation for selected contributions, editorial support, and edition, proofreading, and graphic design handled by the team. TIMELINE: open call closes 13 July 2026; selected contributors announced approx. 15 August 2026; work on contributions approx. 1 September 2026 to 10 January 2027; contributor workshop approx. 15 September 2026; final contribution deadline approx. 10 January 2027. HOW TO APPLY: submit via the Google form. Questions via ecoslay@protonmail.com or Instagram DM @ecoslay_mag.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Chornobyl40: Open Call 2026 (Virtual Residency & International Exhibition)
item.description:
Organisation: Chornobyl40 (gate22 / European Institute of Chornobyl consortium)

Location: Online residency (weekly sessions Tuesdays 14:00-18:00 CET); European touring exhibition 2027 in Madrid, Berlin, Toulouse, and Kyiv. Open internationally.

Award: Residency is free to attend. For the 20 projects selected for production: EUR 500 grant for fully digital works or EUR 1,000 grant for installations requiring physical materials, plus inclusion in the international exhibition. Travel, accommodation, and transport are not covered. No application fee.

Duration: Phase 1 virtual residency September-October 2026 (weekly Tuesday sessions, 30 hours total, minimum 90% attendance). Phase 2 selection and production. Phase 3 European touring exhibition in 2027.

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

Chornobyl40 is a European artistic program, funded by the European Union, exploring the multiple legacies of the Chornobyl disaster not only as a past event but as an ongoing condition shaping the present and future. The open call invites artists, designers, researchers, collectives, and interdisciplinary/hybrid practitioners to engage with Chornobyl as a landscape, a memory, a political reality, and a speculative horizon. This is a call for interpretation, friction, and critical imagination, not illustration. STRUCTURE (three phases): PHASE 1, VIRTUAL RESIDENCY: 40 selected participants, September-October 2026, online, weekly sessions (Tuesdays 14:00-18:00 CET), 30 hours total with minimum 90% attendance; a structured process from idea to concept to pre-production including talks on Chornobyl, ecology, and contaminated territories, encounters with artists and technologists, a Concept Creation Hackathon, an XR Concept Development Workshop (exploratory, non-technical), a Mental Health & Eco-Anxiety Lab (4h), collective discussions, peer feedback, and tailored mentoring. PHASE 2, SELECTION & PRODUCTION: 20 projects selected (based on a video presentation, project documentation, and a live pitch) receive production support. PHASE 3, EXHIBITIONS: the 20 projects tour a European exhibition in 2027 (Madrid, Berlin, Toulouse, Kyiv); artist presence optional but encouraged. WHAT'S OFFERED: for the 40 participants, access to a curated residency, talks, workshops, mentoring, and a European network; for the 20 selected projects, a EUR 500 grant (fully digital works) or EUR 1,000 grant (installations needing physical materials) plus exhibition inclusion (travel, accommodation, and transport not covered). WHO CAN APPLY: artists, designers, researchers, collectives, and interdisciplinary/hybrid practices; open internationally, no age limit, emerging and established profiles equally encouraged. PROJECT THEMES: ecology and post-disaster environments, memory and trauma, geopolitical narratives, invisible forces (radiation, data, systems), technological imaginaries, nuclear energy, and misinformation and perception; projects must relate to Chornobyl (directly or indirectly), be adaptable to multiple exhibition contexts, and consider eco-responsibility; new and adapted existing works both welcome. SELECTION CRITERIA (equally weighted): artistic quality, relevance to the manifesto, eco-responsibility, digital dimension, and emotional and critical impact. TIMELINE: open call launched 19 May 2026; application deadline 12 July 2026; 40 participants announced end of July 2026. HOW TO APPLY: submit in English via the Google Form with a bio/CV link, portfolio link, and a project proposal (PDF, 2 A4 pages maximum). Application is free. Questions: contact@gate22.net.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Doc Society: Climate Story Fund
item.description:
Organisation: Doc Society (Climate Story Unit)

Location: Remote / international. Open to filmmakers of any nationality.

Award: Grants of up to USD 125,000. No application fee.

Duration: Supports late-production of non-fiction projects and the implementation of impact campaigns for completed and in-production projects.

Deadline: 24 Aug 2026

The Doc Society Climate Story Fund, run by Doc Society's Climate Story Unit, provides grants of up to USD 125,000 to support non-fiction projects in late production and the implementation of impact campaigns for both completed and in-production projects. The fund supports compelling independent media storytelling and impact strategies aligned with the Fund's mission around climate justice and biodiversity. This call has no set theme, welcoming applications across all areas aligned with the Fund's mission. Since 2021 the fund has distributed more than USD 4M to over 50 projects, with grantee work reaching distribution through outlets including BBC, POV, Netflix, Hulu, Disney+, Spotify, YouTube, and WhatsApp. WHAT YOU GET: up to USD 125,000 to support non-fiction projects in late production and impact campaigns. WHO IS ELIGIBLE: open to filmmakers of any nationality. DEADLINE: 24 August 2026. TO APPLY: see the fund page and apply via the Doc Society application. Questions: climate@docsociety.org.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Prospect Art: NEW WORK Commission 2027 - "We Come in Pieces"
item.description:
Organisation: Prospect Art

Location: Remote / online. Open to visual artists worldwide; programs (ONE WORK, BROADCAST) run online.

Award: NEW WORK: USD 1,500 commission for one artist, plus a featured website section and social promotion. BROADCAST: USD 125 screening or speaking fee if selected. ONE WORK: a published article (exposure). NOTE: a USD 10 administrative fee applies to apply, with a no-questions-asked waiver available on request before the deadline. PayPal account required to receive funds.

Duration: The commissioned NEW WORK project should be completed within twelve months of selection.

Deadline: 16 Aug 2026

Prospect Art invites visual artists worldwide to apply for the 2027 NEW WORK commission, a USD 1,500 project grant supporting the creation of a new, conceptually driven work responding to the curatorial theme "We Come in Pieces," developed by 2026-2027 Curatorial Fellow Alma Sammel. Through a single application, artists are also considered for ONE WORK (an online series featuring an in-depth conversation centered on a single completed artwork) and BROADCAST (a curated online program of screenings, artist talks, and discussions). THEME: "We Come in Pieces" proposes the Alien as a method for shifting perspective, approaching alienness as a practice of estrangement to encounter the world beyond familiar human-centered narratives. It explores relationships between human and more-than-human worlds, speculative cosmologies, ecological systems, technologies, landscapes, histories, infrastructures, and nonhuman forms of intelligence (satellites and orbital debris, fungal networks, oceans, atmospheric systems, algorithmic intelligences), inviting works that displace the point of listening, seeing, sensing, or knowing. Research-based and conceptually driven practices are especially encouraged. WHAT'S OFFERED: NEW WORK, USD 1,500 to one artist plus a featured website section and social promotion (project completed within 12 months); ONE WORK, a published article and social promotion; BROADCAST, a USD 125 screening or speaking fee and inclusion in a themed program. ELIGIBILITY: professional visual artists working in any medium at any career stage, except those currently enrolled in undergraduate or graduate studio art programs; collectives may apply. Media welcomed include moving image, installation, sculpture, photography, performance, sound, digital media, social practice, and interdisciplinary forms. APPLICATION: submit via the official Google Form with contact information, a project description (250 words), a sketch/visualization (1-3 images), up to 3 links to completed work samples, a statement relating the project to the theme (200 words), a short bio and exhibition history (500 words), and a budget breakdown of the USD 1,500. A USD 10 administrative fee is paid via PayPal (include PayPal name and transaction ID); a fee waiver is available with no explanation by emailing submissions@prospectart.org before the deadline. Writers may email gioj@prospectart.org with samples. DEADLINE: 16 August 2026; results for the NEW WORK grant notified by end of September. INFO SESSION: 5 August 2026 at noon PST.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: New Emergence Art Prize 2026
item.description:
Organisation: New Emergence Art

Location: International; open worldwide. Shortlist shown in an online exhibition.

Award: Prize fund worth over GBP 5,000 across nine awards, including a GBP 1,000 Grand Prize cash award, a two-week NG Art Creative Residency in Provence, France (worth EUR 2,000), a GBP 300 Founder's Prize cash award, and materials or subscription awards from Staedtler UK, Rosemary & Co, GreatArt, Michael Harding, and The Art Ladder. NOTE: there is a paid entry fee.

Duration: One-off prize; shortlisted artists shown in an online exhibition 11 August to 7 September 2026

Deadline: 03 Aug 2026

The New Emergence Art Prize 2026 is an international open call for emerging artists, presented by New Emergence Art. There is no set theme and work may be submitted in any medium. AWARDS: a prize fund worth over GBP 5,000 across nine awards supporting artists through cash prizes, artist materials, mentoring, and a residency, including a GBP 1,000 Grand Prize, a two-week NG Art Creative Residency in Provence, France (worth EUR 2,000), a GBP 300 Founder's Prize cash award, and materials or subscription awards from Staedtler UK, Rosemary & Co, GreatArt, Michael Harding, and The Art Ladder. ELIGIBILITY: open internationally to emerging artists; entrants may submit up to three original artworks that represent the quality, originality and direction of their practice. Final selections are made by an independent judging panel considering concept, execution, originality and impact. Shortlisted artists are featured in an online exhibition running 11 August to 7 September 2026. NOTE: there is a paid entry fee (amount set at the entry step). DEADLINE: 3 August 2026, 23:59 BST. Apply via https://www.newemergenceart.com/apply.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Connections Through Culture Grants 2026 (British Council)
item.description:
Organisation: British Council

Location: International collaboration grant. Each application must involve at least one UK-based partner and at least one partner based in an eligible participating country; each partner must be legally based in the country they apply from throughout the project.

Award: Grants of between GBP 5,000 and GBP 15,000. Free to apply.

Duration: Project-based; develop and deliver collaborative projects across a range of themes and art forms.

Deadline: 12 Aug 2026

Connections Through Culture (CTC) grants are designed to enable meaningful partnerships, cross-cultural exchange and long-term collaboration between the UK and participating countries. In 2026, CTC incorporates the grants programmes formerly known as Biennials Connect (Visual Arts) and Architecture, Design and Fashion Biennials and Festivals Grants, and serves as the British Council's major Arts grants programme for 2026 (in place of International Collaboration Grants). It offers grants of between GBP 5,000 and GBP 15,000 to support creative practitioners, cultural professionals, artists, organisations, festivals, biennials, hubs and collectives to develop and deliver collaborative projects across a wide range of themes and art forms, including applied arts, architecture, curating, dance, design, digital, drawing, education, fashion, film, installation, interdisciplinary work, painting, performance, photography, printmaking, public art, research, sculpture, social practice, sound art, textiles, video, visual arts and writing. ELIGIBILITY: applications must involve collaboration between at least one UK-based partner and at least one partner based in an eligible participating country; each partner must be legally based in the country they are applying from at the time of application and throughout the project activity period. PARTICIPATING COUNTRIES: Albania, Argentina, Australia, Bangladesh, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Colombia, Mainland China, Egypt, Indonesia, Kazakhstan, Kenya, Kosovo, Lebanon, Malaysia, Mexico, Montenegro, New Zealand, Nigeria, North Macedonia, Pakistan, Palestine, Peru, Philippines, Saudi Arabia, Serbia, South Africa, Tanzania, Thailand, Tunisia, Turkey, United Arab Emirates, UK, Ukraine, Uzbekistan, Venezuela, and Vietnam. Eligible applicants include artists and creative practitioners, arts and cultural organisations, festivals and biennials, cultural professionals, creative hubs/networks/collectives, and organisations working across arts, culture and the creative sectors. Note: applications involving projects organised by or taking place as part of Visual Arts, Architecture, Design, Fashion and Craft festivals should be made by the festival or biennial organisation rather than by individual artists, designers or practitioners. No application fee. Deadline 12 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: AIxDESIGN: Grain, Open Call for Critical AI Literacy Recipes
item.description:
Organisation: AIxDESIGN, co-produced with the Netherlands Film Academy

Location: Remote/online; open to educators and practitioners based anywhere

Award: Need-based fee of EUR 150 per recipe for freelance and independent practitioners (email hello@aixdesign.co if the time would be hard to make without it). Your recipe is formatted and credited to you in the first edition of Grain, plus a print copy and an invite to the launch. Published under CC BY-SA 4.0; you keep authorship.

Duration: One-off contribution; Grain publishes March 2027

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

Grain is a cookbook of critical and creative AI literacy exercises for educators across audiovisual and creative practice, co-produced by AIxDESIGN and the Netherlands Film Academy and led by Nadia Piet. The open call collects 'recipes': tried-and-tested group exercises for the classroom or workshop that get people thinking critically and making creatively with AI. Exercises can be hands-on or analog, 15 minutes or a full day, beginner or advanced; the one hard requirement is that someone else can read your recipe and run it themselves. Examples of what might fit: hands-on experiments with specific AI tools (image generators, chatbots, code assistants, video models); analog activities that reveal computational logic through embodied practice; critical readings, watchings, or listenings with structured discussion; provocations, games, or role-plays that get at how AI sees, thinks, or makes; and exercises that help people figure out how they want to relate to AI in their own practice. WHAT YOU GET: your recipe formatted and credited to you in the first edition of Grain, a print copy when it lands, and an invite to the launch. Everything is published under CC BY-SA 4.0 so the work can travel and you keep authorship. FEE: this round offers a need-based fee for freelance and independent practitioners of EUR 150 per recipe; if making the time would be hard without it, email hello@aixdesign.co with your proposal. After this round the call stays open but the fee will not. LIVE SESSION: an optional 30-minute live session on Wednesday 12 August 2026 at 16:00 CEST lets you bring a rough exercise and turn it into a recipe on the spot with the Grain Recipe Converter (online, sign up at luma.com/84w148in). Submissions are open until the end of August 2026; Grain publishes March 2027. Submit via the form; questions to hello@aixdesign.co.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Innovate Grant
item.description:
Organisation: Innovate Artist Grants

Location: Online (applications accepted worldwide)

Award: USD 1,800 unrestricted grant (two awarded per cycle: one to a visual artist, one to a photographer) USD 35 application fee

Duration: One-time unrestricted award; quarterly cycles (Winter, Spring, Summer, Fall)

Deadline: 17 Sep 2026

Innovate Grant awards two unrestricted USD 1,800 grants every quarter, one to a visual artist and one to a photographer, along with up to 18 honorable mentions who are featured and promoted on the organization's website. The award is unrestricted, so recipients can apply the funds toward new projects, materials, studio rent, travel, or other needs. Grant cycles run four times a year (Winter, Spring, Summer, and Fall). Visual artists and photographers aged 18 and older from anywhere in the world, both US and international, are eligible to apply. Applications require contact details, a grant category, and 3 to 10 images of work, plus a USD 35 application fee. The Spring 2026 cycle closed on June 18, 2026, and the Summer 2026 cycle follows on the regular quarterly schedule.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: TED & POSCA Global Artist Residency 2026: Visible Mending
item.description:
Organisation: TED & POSCA (curated by Fine Acts)

Location: Globally distributed; no single location or central studio. Selected artists work within their own communities, with projects unfolding in public space.

Award: Each selected artist receives a total of USD 15,000 (covering artist fees, production costs/community activation, and documentation), plus a production budget and tools from POSCA, creative direction and storytelling support, and visibility through TED and Fine Acts' global platforms.

Duration: One-year residency term; community-rooted public interventions developed in 2026 for implementation in 2027. The initiative runs for multiple years (2027, 2028, 2029), expanding the cohort year by year.

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

TED (the global platform for ideas and storytelling) and POSCA (a creative brand by uni Brands Corporation) are launching a new kind of artist residency: a globally distributed cohort of artists working across different geographies, cultures, and communities, united by a shared drive to shape public life through art. Fine Acts, the external curatorial partner selected by TED & POSCA, leads the curation, creative direction, and coordination. There is no single location and no central studio: selected artists work within their own communities, following a shared creative constraint (a unifying conceptual framework around repair, hope, and beauty), with projects unfolding in public space, community participation as a key part of the work, and process mattering as much as outcome. 2026 THEME, VISIBLE MENDING: inspired in part by Kintsugi (the Japanese practice of repairing broken ceramics with gold), the theme explores repair as a creative and civic act, inviting artists to consider how wounds, ruptures, and imperfections can become sites of transformation, and to make processes of repair visible through public interventions that spark reflection, dialogue, and collective imagination. In 2026, four artists will be selected to develop community-rooted public interventions to be implemented in 2027. AWARD: each selected artist receives a total of USD 15,000 to cover artist fees, production costs/community activation, and documentation, plus a production budget and tools from POSCA, creative direction and storytelling support, and visibility through TED and Fine Acts' global platforms. While the residency term is one year, the initiative will run for multiple years (2027, 2028, 2029), expanding the cohort year by year and building an evolving global archive of civic artistic interventions. Apply by 31 July 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Club Nothing: Community Music Fund
item.description:
Organisation: Nothing

Location: Anywhere in the world.

Award: USD 40,000 fund total; four individuals or collectives each receive USD 10,000 to produce a club event, series, or festival. No application fee.

Duration: Event, series, or festival to take place later in 2026; winners announced late August 2026.

Deadline: 09 Aug 2026

Club Nothing is a global events series and USD 40,000 fund from the tech brand Nothing, supporting organisers, artists, and collectives creating new music experiences. The fund will award four individuals or collectives USD 10,000 each to throw a club event, series, or festival later this year, backing disruptors bringing new sounds and experiences to their communities through nightlife anywhere in the world. Applications are judged by a panel including DJ and producer Manuka Honey (Marissa Malik), Nothing Global Senior Community Manager and Madruga Festival co-founder Erica Travis, Nothing Global Head of Communications Shavone Charles, Dazed Music Editor Solomon Pace-McCarrick, A&R and music-publishing founder James Supreme, and music-industry professional Layla Mustafa Rock. Applications close 9 August 2026; winners announced late August 2026. Learn more and apply at nothing.tech/club-nothing (details also posted at https://nothing.community/d/59214-want-10000-to-throw-a-party). See full terms at nothing.tech/club-nothing/terms-and-conditions.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Eamonn McCabe Bursary 2026 (Royal Photographic Society)
item.description:
Organisation: The Royal Photographic Society (RPS), in partnership with Eamonn McCabe's family and The Guardian

Location: International bursary. Applicants must be aged 30 years or under on the closing date. Interviews held via Zoom.

Award: GBP 3,000 to deliver a photography project on the theme of 'sporting endeavour'. The completed project will be published by The Guardian and the RPS, who will work with the recipient to bring the project to a wider audience.

Duration: The project should be completed within one year of being awarded.

Deadline: 03 Aug 2026

The Eamonn McCabe Bursary, launched in 2024, commemorates the life of photojournalist Eamonn McCabe (1948-2022), who made his name as a sports photographer before moving to wider editorial work, mainly for The Observer and The Guardian, and who supported a generation of photographers from the 1980s to the 2020s. Run by the RPS in partnership with McCabe's family and The Guardian, this international bursary continues that legacy. AWARD: GBP 3,000 to a photographer aged 30 years or under to deliver a photography project responding to the theme of 'sporting endeavour', which can be interpreted as widely as the applicant wishes, though the general approach should be suitable for publication. The project should normally be new, not previously exhibited or published. As well as professional sports, the call particularly encourages projects covering grassroots and community activities (for example, addressing issues such as mental health or supporting displaced communities), and welcomes creative interpretations of the theme. The completed project will be published by The Guardian and the RPS. ELIGIBILITY: applicants must be aged 30 years or under on the closing date. PROCESS: interviews via Zoom in September; recipient announced by the end of September; project completed within one year of award. Apply via the RPS application portal using RPS Account credentials. Closing date 3 August 2026.

Open call details

Location: Francophone zone (OIF member states globally)

Award: Up to EUR 5,000 for artist mobility (travel, accommodation, visas, logistics). Up to EUR 2,500 for circulation of cultural goods (excess baggage, freight, customs). Both can be combined. No application fee.

Duration: Residencies or tours of 5 days to 3 months. Projects must take place between 25 March and 30 November 2026.

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

The OIF Support for Artist Mobility and Circulation of Cultural Goods programme provides grants of up to EUR 5,000 for mobility (travel, accommodation, visa fees, logistics) and up to EUR 2,500 for circulation of cultural goods (excess baggage, freight, customs) within the Francophone zone; both can be combined. ELIGIBILITY: professional artists and established artistic collectives with a demonstrated connection to the Francophone space (by nationality, working language, or artistic collaborations), residing in an OIF member state, with minimum 2 years of experience in the cultural or artistic sector; must directly manage the project (not act as intermediaries); one application per individual per call. ELIGIBLE DISCIPLINES: live performance (music, theatre, dance, comedy, storytelling), cinema/audiovisual, literature/publishing, and visual arts. PRIORITY given to projects promoting gender equality, women artists, creators under 35, and South-South mobility. Projects must take place between 25 March and 30 November 2026. Multiple selection sessions throughout the year; call open until 31 August 2026. Information webinars: 11 March, 13 May, and 8 July 2026 at 3 PM Paris time. Contact: culture.dlc@francophonie.org. item.title: Center for Humane Technology: Emerging Voices in AI and Society Fellowship (Inaugural Cohort)
item.description:
Organisation: Center for Humane Technology (CHT)

Location: Remote, 10 hours/week overlapping with EST required.

Award: $30,000 fixed stipend paid in three milestone-based tranches. No application fee.

Duration: 6 months: September 14, 2026 through March 18, 2027. 15 hours per week.

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

CHT's inaugural Emerging Voices in AI and Society Fellowship places 3 fellows (one per focus area) in a 6-month, part-time program to develop rigorous public-impact research and writing on how AI is reshaping society. Fellows choose from 5 focus areas: (1) AI and Cognition — what AI does to human judgment, critical thinking, and intellectual independence; (2) AI and Relationships — AI's effects on intimacy, companionship, and social bonds; (3) AI and Surveillance — how AI enables monitoring, tracking, and control; (4) AI and Spirituality — AI's intersection with meaning, transcendence, and religious practice; (5) Inside the Machine: AI From a Technologist's Perspective. Up to 2 focus areas per application. DELIVERABLES: one flagship research piece, report, or long-form essay for public impact; 1-3 op-eds (placed in target outlets or CHT Substack); one podcast appearance (Your Undivided Attention or equivalent); cohort meetings and bi-weekly check-ins. SUPPORT: bi-weekly guidance from Senior Director of Strategy and Impact; editorial, communications and graphic design support; media training and PR placement support; CHT email address and Slack access; listing on CHT's public fellows page. WHO SHOULD APPLY: mid-career researchers (psychology, sociology, philosophy, cognitive science, communications, STS), policy analysts, journalists covering tech and society, civil society advocates, or technologists with insider perspective. Strong preference for those with a public writing track record (op-eds, Substack, peer-reviewed work) and some public presence. HOW TO APPLY: 1-2 page research proposal per topic (up to 2), CV/resume (2 pages max), 2-minute video explaining fit, writing sample. Deadline: Sunday 12 July 2026, 11:59pm EST.

Open call details

item.title: Longview Philanthropy: Digital Minds Research Fellowships 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Longview Philanthropy

Location: Worldwide / remote or institutional (grant-eligible countries listed in application form)

Award: $55,000–$120,000/year through a university, or $80,000–$150,000/year direct stipend; plus research budget (10% of salary), conference travel, and compute resources where applicable. No application fee.

Duration: 1–2 years. Second year conditional on continuing to meet programme requirements.

Deadline: 10 Jul 2026

Longview Philanthropy Research Fellowships support scholars pursuing research on the potential consciousness, sentience, moral status, and legal position of AI systems. AMOUNT: $55,000–$120,000/year (through a university, with 10% research budget and standard fringe benefits), or $80,000–$150,000/year (direct stipend, with suggested 5–10% allocation for research and travel). Compute resources available for machine learning research. Invitation and funding to attend the annual Center for Mind, Ethics, and Policy Summit at NYU. ELIGIBILITY: must have or be expected to receive a doctoral degree in computer science, neuroscience, a related technical discipline, law, medicine, or applied social science by September 2027 or fellowship start date (exceptions for equivalent research experience); fellows arrange their own institutional affiliation; fellowship may be conditional on institutional affiliation or attendance at events. SELECTION CRITERIA: demonstrated interest in digital minds and its relation to AI risk; evidence of research promise (publications, academic achievements, references). DURATION: 1–2 years; second year conditional on ongoing programme requirements. AREAS OF INTEREST: introspection, welfare measurement, interpretability, model character, correlates of consciousness, valence research, governance frameworks, legal and political responses to potentially sentient AI. Deadline: 10 July 2026. Required application materials: CV, description of possible research directions (~1,000 words), intended outcomes, what you would do without this fellowship (~150–400 words), career goals (~300 words). Questions: digitalminds-rfp@longview.org. Apply at https://www.longview.org/request-for-proposals-research-and-applied-work-on-digital-minds/.

Open call details

item.title: AdK / EMS Berlin-Stockholm Bilateral Residency Program 2026-27
item.description:
Organisation: Studio für Elektroakustische Musik, Akademie der Künste (Berlin) and Elektronmusikstudion EMS (Stockholm)

Location: Berlin, Germany and Stockholm, Sweden; travel and accommodation covered for stays in both cities.

Award: Travel and accommodation costs for stays in Berlin and Stockholm covered; fees paid for presentation at KONTAKTE Festival 2027 (Berlin) and for presentation in Stockholm. Amount not publicly stated. No application fee.

Duration: Flexible, by agreement; works to be presented at KONTAKTE 2027 in Berlin and at a Stockholm presentation.

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

A new bilateral residency collaboration between the Studio für Elektroakustische Musik at the Akademie der Künste in Berlin and Elektronmusikstudion EMS in Stockholm. Four artists will be selected to develop and present new works in both cities, with presentations at KONTAKTE 2027 in Berlin and at a corresponding event in Stockholm. The program supports new artistic and technical initiatives at the intersection of sound, music, and technology in the field of time-based, performative, and digital arts. ELIGIBLE CATEGORIES: (1) new instrumental and live-electronic works; (2) acousmatic music, computer music, radio art, and other forms of electroacoustic music and sound art; (3) projects from artists of any discipline for whom sound is a central element; (4) curatorial, publishing, and educational projects in sound-based art practice, particularly those relating in content to the AdK Studio or EMS; (5) independent research in acoustics, computer science, or musicology -- specifically software development, spatialization, or network technologies. Open to individuals and groups. Project duration is flexible and arranged by agreement. SUPPORT: travel and accommodation costs for Berlin and Stockholm covered; presentation fees paid for both venues. Selection is made by the director and advisory board of the Studio für Elektroakustische Musik and the director of EMS; decision is final with no legal entitlement to funding. Incomplete or late applications are not considered. This call supplements the Studio's regular residency program (separate deadline June 30, 2026 for July-December 2026 period). Apply exclusively via the online form at https://nc.adk.de/index.php/apps/forms/s/HD2nxbzqwgeMHWN6xHg9bQrA. Questions: estudio@adk.de. Deadline: 31 July 2026.

item.title: Democracy Stories Lab: Open Call for Visual Storytellers
item.description:
Organisation: People Powered and Democracy Narratives Alliance

Location: Worldwide; remote. Global screening event for winners.

Award: USD $1,000 for each of 4 winners; USD $500 for each of 5 honorable mentions. Plus amplification across People Powered and Democracy Narratives Alliance networks and feature in a global screening event. No application fee.

Duration: One-time award.

Deadline: 14 Sep 2026

The Democracy Stories Lab, an initiative of People Powered (a global hub for participatory democracy) in partnership with the Democracy Narratives Alliance, invites creators worldwide to submit visual content -- short videos, illustrations, or graphics -- that tells a compelling story about democracy, voice, fairness, belonging, and community power. Content can be original or already published, as long as it is the applicant's own work and engages with narrative frameworks from the Democracy Narratives Alliance's research on 'How to Talk About Democracy.' Tone is open; serious and comedic approaches are both welcome. AWARDS: 4 winning entries receive USD $1,000 each; 5 honorable mentions receive USD $500 each; all winners receive amplification across People Powered and Democracy Narratives Alliance communications networks and are featured in a global screening event. WHO CAN APPLY: individual creators and small teams anywhere in the world; especially welcomes creators who reach audiences outside usual democracy circles, who come from communities traditionally with less of a say in public discourse, and who work in civic tech, advocacy, community organising, comedy, art, illustration, games, or lifestyle content. Applications open 14 July 2026 via the Democracy Stories Lab page at https://www.peoplepowered.org/news-content/announcing-the-democracy-stories-lab; subscribe to the People Powered newsletter to be notified when the form opens. Submission deadline: 14 September 2026. Winners announced October-November 2026. Note: the initiative was itself voted into existence through People Powered's participatory budgeting process (PP Decides).

item.title: Revitalizing Rituals 2026 Open Call
item.description:
Organisation: Homo Faber Alapítvány (Homo Faber Foundation)

Location: Bhar Lazreg, Tunisia and Somogy County, Hungary; multi-stage in-person residency across both sites.

Award: EUR 6,000 gross artist fee and production budget; international travel and per diems covered; on-site accommodation provided. No application fee.

Duration: Multi-stage residency: Placemaking Camp (1 week, November 2026, Hungary); Storytellers Camp (1 week, March 2027, Tunisia); Residency Period (minimum 1 month, April-July 2027); public presentation (August 2027); Closing Event (3 days, winter/early spring 2028, Hungary).

Deadline: 19 Jul 2026

Revitalizing Rituals invites artists from all disciplines -- sound, movement, visual arts, installation, performance, and transdisciplinary practices -- to join a research-creation journey across two landscapes: Bhar Lazreg in Tunisia and the wetlands of Somogy County in Hungary. The project explores rituals as living forms of belonging: reconnecting communities, land, memory, food, ecological reciprocity, and the more-than-human world. Selected artists work with local communities as co-participants in a shared inquiry into moving from extraction toward reciprocity. Organised by Homo Faber Alapítvány (Hungary). AMOUNT: EUR 6,000 gross artist fee and production budget; international travel and per diems covered; on-site accommodation provided. ELIGIBILITY: minimum 5 years of active artistic practice; portfolio reflecting interest in situated or relational work; no formal degree required; working language is English (local language navigation valued); must be available for the full residency cycle including all international travel; must be able to legally receive the fee and invoice the organising partners from country of residence. Strongly encourages applications from historically marginalised or underrepresented voices. KEY DATES: Application Deadline 19 July 2026 (midnight) -- note the open call PDF also references a 5 July date; the officially promoted deadline is 19 July; Online Q&A 29 June 2026, 14:00 Tunisia / 15:00 CET (register at https://forms.gle/brnZvXvFe4JB8zL26); Online Conversations with shortlisted artists: mid July to mid August 2026; Selection Results: 31 August 2026; Placemaking Camp: 1 week, end of November 2026, Hungary; Storytellers Camp: 1 week, March 2027, Tunisia; Residency Period: minimum 1 month (splittable), April to July 2027; Final Presentation: August 2027; Closing Event: 3 days, winter/early spring 2028, Hungary. Apply via the open call form linked at https://homofaber.hu/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Artist-Open-Call-FINAL-in-the-RR-form.pdf.

Open call details

item.title: Jamverse Jam (Protocolized worldbuilding contest)
item.description:
Organisation: Protocolized (Summer of Protocols)

Location: Online / Worldwide

Award: One USD 1,000 grand prize plus at least 10 additional USD 200 prizes; winning artifacts are published on the Jamverse website. No entry fee.

Duration: One-off contest; submit up to three artifacts by 31 July 2026

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

Protocolized's fourth contest invites artists and writers (storytellers, poets, comic artists) to build, extend, and connect the Jamverse, a shared near-future science fiction universe of four intersecting story cycles (The Stockton Chronicles, Legends and Ledgers, UET-Trainverse, and Zoothesia). ELIGIBILITY: open to anyone; you may submit up to three in-universe worldbuilding artifacts, and the same creator can win multiple prizes. REQUIREMENTS: each artifact must be under 1,000 words (200-700 is the sweet spot) or a visual equivalent (comic panel, poster, advertisement, etc.) and must include at least one element drawn from one of the four sub-world 'toy boxes', a surprising 'traffic jam' (an emergent, ah-ha consequence of a technology or protocol), and a relation to the theme of Stigmergy (indirect coordination through modifying a shared environment). Artifact types can include comic panels, job postings, poems, forum threads, historic documents, or encyclopedia entries. APPLY: submit each artifact as a public Google Doc via the contest form, including the artifact, a one-paragraph explanation of the worlds/toy-box components used and how the piece relates to stigmergy and explores a traffic jam, and, if AI was used, documentation of your AI/LLM protocol. PRIZES: one USD 1,000 grand prize and at least 10 USD 200 prizes, all published on the Jamverse website. DEADLINE: 31 July 2026. Contest rules, the current Jamverse stories, and starter materials are on the Jamverse website.

Open call details

The eidolon Grant is an international programme, presented annually, for projects that explore, promote and conserve vernacular photography. It is open to artists, academics, professionals, researchers, collectors and vernacular photography enthusiasts whose past work and proposed project is centred around the image heritage of everyday photography. item.title: Scaling AI Safety for a Multi-Agent World (Joint Funding Call)
item.description:
Organisation: Schmidt Sciences, Google DeepMind, the Advanced Research & Invention Agency (ARIA), the Cooperative AI Foundation, and Google.org

Location: Global; open worldwide, with collaborations across geographic boundaries encouraged.

Award: Two funding tiers (applicants may apply to either or both), projects 1 to 2 years. Tier 1: up to USD 300,000 for exploratory research, pilot studies, or focused technical investigations. Tier 2: USD 300,000 to USD 1,000,000 for more ambitious or collaborative projects. For projects funded by Schmidt Sciences, indirect costs must be at or below 10%. No application fee.

Duration: 1 to 2 years

Deadline: 08 Aug 2026

A joint philanthropic funding call by Schmidt Sciences, Google DeepMind, ARIA, the Cooperative AI Foundation and Google.org to catalyse the foundational scientific research needed to understand, evaluate and control risks emerging from large-scale ecosystems of interacting AI agents deployed by multiple actors (multi-principal, multi-agent settings). The call argues that focusing only on the safety and alignment of individual models is insufficient, and seeks system-level approaches. RESEARCH AGENDA (four clusters): (1) Sandboxes and Testbeds, realistic, reproducible multi-agent environments for studying populations of frontier-model agents; (2) The Science of Agent Networks, how collective capabilities emerge and scale, how networks fail, and how dangerous population-level properties can be detected; (3) Strengthening Agent Infrastructure, evaluating and stress-testing primitives such as identity, verifiability, reputation, communication and commitment; (4) Multi-Agent Oversight and Control, detection, attribution, security and intervention methods to keep deployed agent populations safe at scale. Proposals may target one cluster or span several, with priority on depth over breadth and on realistic sandboxes and testbeds. OUT OF SCOPE: single-agent safety, capability advancement without a clear safety motivation, toy systems, naive application of pre-existing solutions, commercial product development, and purely conceptual/policy work. ELIGIBILITY: individual researchers, research teams, research institutions and multi-institution collaborations across universities, national laboratories, institutes and nonprofit research organisations; open globally. Informational webinars on 30 June and 23 July 2026; notification of decisions in autumn 2026. Proposals due 8 August 2026 by 11:59pm Anywhere on Earth. Contact: multiagentsafety@schmidtsciences.org. Apply at https://schmidtsciences.smapply.io/prog/scaling_ai_safety_for_a_multi_agent_world/.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Pulitzer Center: AI Accountability Fellowships 2026-2027
item.description:
Organisation: Pulitzer Center

Location: Worldwide; reporters may be based anywhere and the fellowship is remote. Proposals from the Global South and from journalists representing a broad array of backgrounds are encouraged.

Award: Up to USD 25,000 per fellow, paid in three installments (up to USD 20,000 for reporting and USD 5,000 for engagement activities); covers records requests, travel, data analysis and engagement costs. Freelancers may budget up to one third of the total as a stipend; newsrooms are expected to pay staff salaries. No application fee.

Duration: 10 months, expected September 2026 to July 2027

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

The AI Accountability Fellowships support journalists working on in-depth AI accountability stories that examine how governments and corporations use predictive, generative and surveillance technologies to guide decisions in policing, medicine, social welfare, criminal justice, hiring and more. Designed for reporters from all beats, desks and formats, the fellowship asks applicants to propose a concrete reporting project (with evidence of pre-reporting) that uses approaches such as data analysis, records requests and shoe-leather reporting to examine the real-world impact of algorithms. For the first time this year, the fellowship also includes funding, mentorship and training to develop and execute an impact/engagement plan to reach strategic audiences. SUPPORT: up to USD 25,000 per fellow (up to USD 20,000 reporting plus USD 5,000 engagement), paid in three installments; freelancers may budget up to one third as a stipend, while newsrooms cover staff salaries. Fellows also receive mentors, specialized training, pro bono legal and public records support, and a lasting community of peers. ELIGIBILITY: staff or freelance journalists across print, radio, video and multimedia, able to work collaboratively; reporters may be based anywhere (fellowship is remote). Experience reporting on AI is not required, but a track record of in-depth, impactful reporting and investigative/data/explanatory experience is valued. Small teams may apply with a designated lead Fellow. REQUIREMENTS: a mandatory monthly 1.5-2 hour meeting, at least one community call, engagement with other fellows, and sharing of methodologies and lessons learned; fellowship communication, meetings and training are in English, but fellows may publish in any language. APPLY: submit a 500-word statement of purpose, a 500-word project description with reporting plan, an engagement plan, a budget, three links to recent work, a letter of commitment from a publishing media organization (or editor support letter for staff), three references and a CV. Funded with support from the MacArthur Foundation, Luminate, Omidyar Network and others. Contact: reacheditorial@pulitzercenter.org. Deadline: 12 July 2026, 11:59pm EDT.

Open call details

item.title: IWMF: The Kari Howard Fund for Narrative Journalism 2026
item.description:
Organisation: International Women's Media Foundation (IWMF)

Location: Worldwide; projects reported and published in English anywhere in the world. Open to women and nonbinary journalists.

Award: Grants average around USD 5,000 (based on previous years). No application fee.

Duration: All reporting and publishing must be completed within six months of the award

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

Established in memory of editor Kari Howard, who championed narrative journalism that wove the music of everyday life into stories illuminating the most important issues of the day. Offered by Kari's family and friends together with the International Women's Media Foundation, the fund supports narrative journalism projects. Grants average around USD 5,000 (based on previous years). ELIGIBILITY: open to women and nonbinary journalists. Applicants may be a print journalist or a print journalist leading a multi-media team, and may be freelance or staff; they may apply individually or as part of a multi-format team. Professional journalism must be the applicant's primary profession, with three or more years of professional experience (internships do not count). Applicants must show proof of interest from an editor or a proven track record of publication in prominent media outlets. Applicants must apply and publish in English; projects published anywhere in the world are eligible, and stories may be published digitally (not necessarily in a newspaper or magazine). Any multi-format reporting must supplement a printed project. All reporting and publishing must be completed within six months of the award. Applicants receive a decision by early September. DEADLINE: 12 July 2026, 11:59pm EST.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Spencer Foundation: Vision Grants 2026 (Research Planning Grants)
item.description:
Organisation: Spencer Foundation

Location: Worldwide; PI must be affiliated with a non-profit or public/governmental institution (US 501(c)(3) or non-US equivalent) that will serve as the administering organization. Proposals accepted from the US and internationally; English only; budgets in US Dollars.

Award: USD 75,000 total per grant; no indirect cost charges permitted. No application fee.

Duration: 12 to 18 months (research planning grant)

Deadline: 12 Aug 2026

Vision Grants are research planning grants that give scholars and their collaborators the time, space, resources and support to plan a large-scale study or program of research focused on transforming education systems toward greater equity. Rather than a fully fleshed-out research plan, the proposal is an invitation to think forward about what research is needed to transform education systems toward equity and how that systems change will happen, identifying the system(s) targeted and the specific levers the team thinks must be engaged. Grants bring together a team for 12 to 18 months to collaboratively develop ambitious, large-scale, cross-disciplinary research projects co-designed with practitioners, policymakers, communities and other partners. Awarded teams also join a cohort learning program held in person in Chicago. ELIGIBILITY: PIs and Co-PIs must have appropriate experience or an earned doctorate in an academic discipline or a terminal degree in a professional field; graduate students may be on the team but cannot be PI or Co-PI. The PI must be affiliated with a non-profit or public/governmental institution willing to serve as the administering organization (colleges, universities, school districts, research facilities, or other 501(c)(3) non-profits, or non-US equivalents); the Spencer Foundation does not award grants directly to individuals. Open to applicants in the US and internationally; proposals in English, budgets in USD. PIs and Co-PIs may apply even with another active Spencer grant or proposal in review, but may not be part of more than one Vision Grant proposal. Note: a Vision Grant is a prerequisite for applying to Spencer's Transformative Research Grant program (TRG, USD 3.5 million), though receiving one does not guarantee a TRG. PROCESS: applications opened 4 June 2026. A required Intent to Apply form (max 200 words, non-binding) is due 12 August 2026 at 12:00 PM noon Central time; the Full Proposal (2000-word narrative) is due 16 September 2026 at 12:00 PM noon Central time. Program contact: Jasmine Knetl, visiongrants@spencer.org.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Longview Philanthropy: Digital Minds Career Development Fellowships 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Longview Philanthropy (in collaboration with Future Impact Group)

Location: Worldwide / remote

Award: Stipend calibrated to the duration of the award, cost of living and the recipient's prior experience, plus additional funding for research or logistics on a case-by-case basis. No application fee.

Duration: Variable; calibrated per award

Deadline: 10 Jul 2026

Funding to develop a career working on the potential consciousness, sentience, moral status or welfare of artificial intelligence systems (digital minds). The fellowships aim to grow a new generation of researchers, policymakers, communicators, entrepreneurs and practitioners working on digital minds and AI sentience issues. EXAMPLE CANDIDATES: a current or planned PhD or other degree student seeking financial support to study digital minds issues; a technical AI researcher who wants to explore research topics, attend events and meet organisations in the space in order to select a high-impact role; or a communications professional pivoting into digital minds work (for example running surveys or building experts' platforms). SUPPORT: a stipend calibrated to award duration, cost of living and prior experience, plus additional case-by-case funding for research or logistics. APPLY by 10 July 2026. Questions: digitalminds-rfp@longview.org.

Award: Research grant of up to USD 6,000. No application fee.

Duration: Research project (grant period per programme rules)

The IOC Olympic Studies Centre supports PhD students and early-career academics conducting scholarly research on the Olympic Movement, its history and ideals, the athletes, the Olympic Games and their impact on contemporary society and culture. ELIGIBILITY: current postgraduate students enrolled in a PhD programme within the human and/or social sciences, with Olympism, the Olympic Movement or the Olympic Games as at least one research focus; also academic staff and postdoctoral fellows who completed their doctorate (or equivalent highest degree) in or after 2024. item.title: King Baudouin Foundation: Ernest Solvay Fund 2026 (Call for Projects)
item.description:

Location: Organisations implementing a project within a 100 km radius of a Solvay industrial, R&I or administrative site

Award: Up to EUR 10,000 per initiative. Funding covers only expenses incurred after the results are announced (mid-December 2026); already-incurred expenses are not reimbursed. No application fee.

Duration: Project implemented after mid-December 2026 announcement

Deadline: 30 Sep 2026

The Ernest Solvay Fund provides financial support for local-impact initiatives in three areas: scientific education (science, technology, engineering and mathematics), planet progress (environmental initiatives) and better life (community well-being). ELIGIBILITY: organisations submitting a project must implement it within a 100 km radius of a Solvay industrial, R&I or administrative site. FUNDING: up to EUR 10,000 per initiative; the Fund covers expenses incurred only after the results are announced (mid-December 2026) and will not reimburse expenses incurred beforehand. APPLY: create or log in to an account at https://candidate.kbs-frb.be and complete the online application form (it can be saved and completed in several stages). Applications are open 15 April 2026 to 30 September 2026; results announced mid-December 2026.

item.title: Who Let The Docs Out: Documentary Production Grants (2026 Cycles)
item.description:
Organisation: Who Let The Docs Out

Location: Remote program (monthly virtual sessions); documentary projects

Award: Stage-based funding: Research USD 8,000; Development/Sizzle USD 15,000; Production USD 50,000; Post-Production USD 50,000; Impact Campaign USD 30,000. No application fee.

Duration: Required program commitment of roughly three months of monthly virtual sessions during the awarded cycle

Deadline: 10 Jul 2026

Who Let The Docs Out provides funding across five stages of documentary production: Research (USD 8,000), Development/Sizzle (USD 15,000), Production (USD 50,000), Post-Production (USD 50,000), and Impact Campaign (USD 30,000). Funding is organized into thematic funds such as the Coexistence Documentary Fund and the Automation & Humanity Documentary Fund. There are three funding cycles per year, each with its own application window and the documentary stages it supports. 2026 SPRING CYCLE (Research & Development Grants): applications open 16 February 2026, close 16 March 2026; notification mid-April 2026; required program commitment May-July (monthly virtual sessions). 2026 SUMMER CYCLE (Research, Development & Production Grants): applications open 5 June 2026, close 10 July 2026; notification 15 August 2026; required program commitment September-November (monthly virtual sessions). 2026 AUTUMN CYCLE (Research, Development & Post-Production Grants): applications open 1 October 2026, close 15 November 2026; notification 15 December 2026; required program commitment January-March (monthly virtual sessions). Awarded filmmakers must commit to monthly virtual sessions during their cycle. The deadline shown here is the currently open Summer Cycle close date (10 July 2026); see the funder's site for full eligibility and thematic-fund details, or contact hello@wholethedocsout.org.

Open call details

item.title: Doha Film Festival 2026: Call for Submissions (Short Films)
item.description:
Organisation: Doha Film Institute (DFI)

Location: Doha, Qatar (festival 19-27 November 2026; at least one film representative must attend in person)

Award: Cash awards (shared between producer and director for Best Film prizes). International Short: Best Film USD 20,000, Best Director USD 12,000, Best Performance USD 7,000. Ajyal: Best Feature Film USD 35,000, Best Short Film USD 12,000. Made in Qatar: Best Film USD 15,000, Best Director USD 10,000, Best Performance USD 5,000. International Feature (invitation only): Best Narrative USD 75,000, Best Documentary USD 50,000, Best Artistic Achievement USD 45,000, Best Performance USD 15,000. No entry fee.

Duration: Festival takes place 19-27 November 2026

Deadline: 01 Aug 2026

The Doha Film Festival (Doha Film Institute), running 19-27 November 2026, is accepting submissions, which are open for SHORT FILMS ONLY (feature films are selected by invitation only). Submittable competitions: the International Short Film Competition (new, daring short-form work, all genres, maximum 20 minutes, MENA premiere required) and the Ajyal Film Competition short-film category (youth-focused films, maximum 20 minutes, Middle East premiere excluding North Africa, curated with a jury of young people aged 16-25). The Made in Qatar section is open to narrative and documentary short films connected to Qatar (Qatari national or resident writer/director/producer, majority shot in Qatar, or storyline centred on Qatar), requiring Qatar premiere status. The International Feature Film Competition (documentary and narrative, 60+ minutes, MENA premiere) is by invitation only and does not accept submissions. All non-English-language works must have English subtitles and a time-coded English dialogue list; as Qatar is bilingual, films must be accessible in Arabic and English (the Festival can provide Arabic subtitles). KEY DATES: short-film submissions open 10 May 2026; deadline for International Short and Ajyal Short Competitions is 1 August 2026; Made in Qatar deadline is 1 September 2026; invited filmmakers notified by 1 October 2026. No entry fee. Submit via the entry form with a private (password-protected) link to the film. Enquiries: entries@dohafilm.com.

Open call details

item.title: AXS Film Fund Open Call 2026
item.description:
Organisation: AXS Film Fund

Location: Remote (worldwide; English-language application; non-English projects must have English subtitles)

Award: Up to $10,000 per grant; up to 5 creators awarded (aim to support 3-5 artists in this round). Application Assistance Stipends also available on a first-come, first-served basis to hire an application assistant. No application fee.

Duration: Project-based (any stage of production: pre-production, production, post-production, or distribution)

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

Grants from the AXS Film Fund supporting documentary and nonfiction new media creators, particularly those who identify as living with a disability and especially from underserved communities, though applications are welcome from all creators regardless of background. Uses the Nonfiction Core Application led by the IDA and Sundance Institute. PROJECT ELIGIBILITY: feature-length documentary films (45 minutes or longer; experimental nonfiction accepted) or nonfiction new media projects with a film/video component; projects in any stage of production; non-English projects must have English subtitles and the application must be completed in English. INELIGIBLE: fiction projects (including narrative fiction based on a true story); projects where applicants are hired, employed, or commissioned by another entity; incomplete applications or those missing supporting materials; late submissions. APPLICANT ELIGIBILITY: 18 or older; not currently enrolled in a degree-granting program; individual applicants (fiscal sponsors permitted); applicant must be the director or producer of the project. SELECTION CRITERIA: strength of proposal and artistic approach; feasibility; ethics and accountability; completeness; and meeting eligibility requirements. ACCESSIBILITY: alternative application methods available (email filmfund@axslab.org by 24 July 2026); an Application Assistance Stipend is offered for 2026 to allow applicants to hire an application assistant, available first-come, first-served (not guaranteed). TIMELINE: open call opens 1 June 2026 (12:00 AM EST); deadline 31 July 2026 (11:59 PM EST). Apply via SurveyMonkey Apply.

Open call details

item.title: MUUS Collection: Research Fellowship 2026-2027 (Remote, USA)
item.description:
Organisation: MUUS Collection

Location: Remote / hybrid; archive visits to Tenafly, New Jersey, USA

Award: USD 20,000 grant plus full coverage of travel costs to the archive. No application fee.

Duration: One year, November 2026 - November 2027

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

MUUS Collection (an American 20th-century photography collection that preserves, researches and reveals work from the archives it owns and represents) launches its first Research Fellowship, inviting a curator or academic to spend a year with the archives to develop an exhibition or publication concept offering a new perspective. The Research Fellow examines physical works and ephemera (journals, contact sheets, cameras, the totality of each photographer's collection) and benefits from the new MUUSEUM online research portal. PROJECT PERIOD: November 2026 to November 2027 (project completes November 2027). ELIGIBILITY: minimum five years professional experience at museums, galleries, universities or similar cultural institutions; international candidates eligible but fluency in English (written and spoken) is required; candidates must be willing to travel to Tenafly, New Jersey for up to a week (mutually agreed dates) and be available for remote collaboration with the archive team and advisory board. Candidates from underrepresented or marginalised communities are encouraged to apply. AWARD: USD 20,000 grant plus full coverage of travel costs to the archive. ANNOUNCEMENT: winner decided in October 2026 and announced at Paris Photo. DEADLINE: 31 July 2026.

Annual grants from the Altercine Foundation (Montreal) to support the production of a documentary project, awarding roughly CAD 10,000 and CAD 5,000 each year. item.title: PhotoVogue Global Open Call 2026: Brave New Visions - Creativity as Rebellion
item.description:
Organisation: PhotoVogue (Conde Nast)

Location: Online (global)

Award: $12,000 in total grants split across three artists: a $6,000 Outstanding Vision Grant, a $4,000 Vision Grant, and a $2,000 Rising Voice Grant (emerging), plus presentation at the next PhotoVogue Festival, potential publication across Vogue's global editions, and PhotoVogue Virtual Portfolio Reviews. Free to submit.

Duration: One-off open call

Deadline: 11 Sep 2026

PhotoVogue's global open call invites photographers and video makers to use image-making as a form of rebellion - to challenge indifference, disrupt conventions and expand visual storytelling. It asks not for a theme but for a position in relation to the world. Open to photography, video and multimedia projects across all genres (fashion, documentary, portraiture, fine art, experimental). Three artists share $12,000 in grants ($6,000 Outstanding Vision, $4,000 Vision, $2,000 Rising Voice for an emerging artist), with festival presentation, potential Vogue publication and portfolio reviews. ELIGIBILITY: open to all artists worldwide aged 18+; submissions are free; previous PhotoVogue applicants may submit a new project. SUBMIT: a series of up to 15 images, or images plus video for multimedia, and/or a 60-second trailer, via Picter. IMPORTANT: AI-generated works are NOT eligible. Open 14 May to 11 September 2026 (11:59 PM CET).

item.title: CRP Research Fellowship Programme 2026
item.description:
Organisation: CRP Research Fellowship Programme

Location: Participating member countries (host institution must be in a different participating country from the applicant's home institution)

Award: Travel lump-sum allowance plus return economy airfare, a weekly subsistence allowance of EUR 600 or EUR 650 (depending on host-country cost of living), and a fixed EUR 165 terminal allowance. Not covered: insurance, visa fees, lab/bench fees, family travel, and personal or dependents' costs.

Duration: 6 to 26 weeks (short-term research placement abroad)

Deadline: 10 Sep 2026

Short-term international research fellowship for scientists seeking placements at host institutions in participating member countries. Fellowships last 6 to 26 weeks and support international scientific collaboration, research mobility, knowledge exchange and cross-border partnerships. PRIORITY THEMES: sustainable agricultural productivity, climate action, environmental protection, biodiversity conservation, soil health, water resource sustainability, knowledge innovation, sustainable livestock systems, fisheries development and aquaculture sustainability. FUNDING: a travel lump sum, return economy airfare, a weekly subsistence allowance (EUR 600 or EUR 650 by host-country cost of living) and a EUR 165 terminal allowance; insurance, visa fees, lab/bench fees and family or personal costs are not covered. ELIGIBILITY: employed by or affiliated with an institution in a participating country; proposed host institution in a different participating country; at least 4 years of postdoctoral experience (exceptional candidates with equivalent expertise and a strong publication record may also be considered). Applicants must not already hold a position at the host institution, need employer approval and assurance of continued employment or affiliation after the fellowship, and previous fellows may reapply only after a 5-year gap. Applicants confirm country eligibility, secure a host institution and collaboration, prepare a research proposal (objective, scientific relevance, methodology, expected outcomes, 6-26 week timeline, collaboration benefits) and submit supporting documents (CV, publication list, employer approval, host acceptance letter, proof of affiliation).

Open call details

item.title: Day Job: Short Film Fund 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Day Job Films

Location: Worldwide applicants; all production must take place in the UK

Award: Production budget of up to GBP 7,000, with full creative control and no producer or external interference. No application fee.

Duration: Per short film project

Deadline: 10 Aug 2026

Short film competition from Day Job Films created to give auteur directors full creative freedom, awarding a production budget of up to GBP 7,000 to make a short film with no producer interference or commercial agenda. Open to animation, live-action and documentary projects. Runs in three rounds: (1) a pitch deck outlining concept, story, key characters and visual style; (2) a full script for those who progress; and (3) an interview to discuss the vision and execution. A panel of industry professionals judges the pitch deck, script and interview, with at least one team member reading each pitch deck and script in full. The winner receives full creative control over the project. ELIGIBILITY: applicants can be from anywhere worldwide, but all production must take place in the UK; applicants must have a producer on board who is separate from the director/writer. Applicants may optionally include a plan for adapting the script into a feature-length film (this does not affect the outcome). Submissions open February 10, 2026 and close August 10, 2026. Enquiries: hello@dayjobfilms.com.

item.title: AMR: 2026 Paper of the Future Prize
item.description:
Organisation: Association for Mathematical Research (AMR)

Location: Worldwide (digital submission)

Award: USD 10,000 prize to the winner; runner-up recognitions at committee discretion. The committee reserves the right to withhold the prize if no submissions meet the standards.

Duration: Project-based

Deadline: 01 Sep 2026

USD 10,000 prize from the Association for Mathematical Research for serious experimentation in how mathematicians communicate with one another, beyond the static PDF. Submissions should demonstrate communicative capabilities fundamentally unavailable in a linear paper: interactive exploration of parameter spaces in differential equations, dynamic visualisation of group actions or geometric structures, multi-perspective representations of algebraic or number-theoretic objects, nonlinear navigation of proof architectures or dependency graphs, embedded computation as part of exposition, or similar. ELIGIBILITY: no nationality, institutional or career restriction stated; the focus is mathematical depth and communicative innovation. This is NOT for popularisation, production polish or short-form video content - it is about novel research-to-research communication. EVALUATION CRITERIA: mathematical depth and rigor; conceptual insight enabled by the medium; communicative innovation beyond static exposition; scalability and reproducibility; transformative potential for research communication. PROCESS: initial submissions are a short public YouTube concept demonstration (with the hashtag #AMRPotF and a concise written explanation of the mathematical substance and communicative innovation in the video description), with the YouTube link emailed to PotFPrize@amathr.org. Finalists are asked to provide a fully accessible prototype suitable for hosting or linking within AMR Reviews; the winning submission will be published in AMR Reviews as an interactive exposition. Selection committee: Mohammed Abouzaid, Benson Farb, Alex Kontorovich, Akshay Venkatesh, Maryna Viazovska.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).
